It's churlish of me to say, but this is part of why I didn't implement file handling in gabbi-run in its earlier incarnations: The reporting handling gets more complicated, especially if a _large_ number of files are being used. Even if each suite prints out its name it could have scrolled off the screen ages ago.

So to fix this properly a few different changes are probably necessary:

* we need to preserve the suite name when creating the suite, by change the `test_base_name` in the call to `test_suite_from_dict` from `input` to the filename (if not using `stdin`).
* consider aggregating the success or failure of each suite for a final summary report, since we're in a loop anyway, this ought to be doable

Of course this all goes to pooh if some bright bulb decided that gabbi-run should be multi-process and results need to be accumulated for runner-per-file. _Please_ nobody go that route. If you need that use `driver.build_tests` and a concurrent testrunner.

Hey, thanks for the report. I could reproduce the problem and looked further into the issue. It turns out ISO C does not guarantee casts from `void*` to function pointers to succeed, since the standard assumes `void*` is a different type (data) than a function pointer and these can have different representations. [Steve Summit explains that better than me, in his Stackoverflow answer](http://stackoverflow.com/a/31527867/969534).

There are workarounds to trick the compiler (see SO answer), or your solution which only shifts the problem to the loader function. 

The only real solution is to compile the relevant files (glad) without `-pedantic`, simply because casting from `void*` to a function pointer is not ISO C semantics.

Apart from that ProhibitUnusedVariable works very well for Neomake: https://github.com/neomake/neomake/pull/1988.
I have used `ignore_pattern` `'^s:'` to work around this for now.

@dabeaz FWIW, I do actually think trio is way better than threads for most people, especially beginners. You have `await` syntax to help you keep track of schedule/cancel-points, you can actually cancel things (!), exceptions don't get lost, control-C works, you don't have to start cargo-culting `daemon=True` flags around the place to get your program to stop freezing during shutdown, etc. Obviously there are limitations -- especially the immature ecosystem -- but the basic architecture is wayyy easier to deal with IMO, and ecosystems can grow over time.

`timeout_after` and friends could return the associated cancel token, and their exceptions could carry it, allowing using to do things like check whether an exception was due to a particular timeout, and also for code like
```python
async with ignore_after(10) as token:
    ...
if token.is_set():
    # the timeout fired
    ...
```

(This is related to something I've been meaning to propose for a bit, actually. There are basically three things people might want to do after a timeout has fired: (a) ignore, (b) catch and handle, (c) throw an exception like "I give up (btw it was because of a timeout)". Right now we use `ignore_after` for ignore and `timeout_after` for catch-and-handle and throw-an-exception, but this ends up being kinda suboptimal because catch-and-handle requires complex syntax with multiple nesting, and the mixture of the two of them creates the mess where we end up needing three exception types to avoid mixing up these two cases. If we switch to using the pattern above, so that there's one context manager used for the ignore and catch-and-handle cases, and a different one used for throw-an-exception, then these both go away.)

The kqueue structure is used in determining which task will run next in the kernel scheduler.  Right now it's a deque(), but it could be changed to something else should priority queuing be added.

_wake_queue and _sleeping are internal data structures used for a different purpose than task scheduling decisions.  _wake_queue contains tasks awakened by the completion of Futures in separate execution threads.  _sleeping is used to manage time (and in the current version of Curio is replaced by a special TimeQueue class).

Absolutely. In fact, looking at docopt, it appears that each language project has a different set of authors and contributors. So it's more like an affiliation of separate projects based on a core design idea, rather than a single group of people writing docopt over and over again in different languages.

So if some folks want to take a stab at recreating Curio in Rust, it sounds like they should just go ahead and do it. If it works out, then perhaps down the line the two implementations (original and Rust flavor) can be brought together under a single GitHub organization, like how docopt did it.

There is a mapping table in the docs that shows how Zenpy translates the operators Zendesk uses  - http://docs.facetoe.com.au/zenpy.html#searching-the-api, and a great article provided by Zendesk that shows the available operators and how they can be used - https://support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/203663226.

You're right - addict is a data structure. It's not exactly the fact that things get duplicated, but the fact that, if we do the above, we clone objects and discard the originals. Hence, the reference to the object is lost. This still happens with addicts constructor as of today, but I'm leaning more towards taking it away entirely, than extending it.

```
>>> a = [1, 2, 3]
>>> addict_dict = Dict({'a': a})
>>> normal_dict = dict({'a': a})
>>> addict_dict.['a'][2] = 4
>>> a
[1, 2, 3]
>>> normal_dict['a'][2] = 4
>>> a
[1, 2, 4]
```

Right now, Markus supports something like this:

```
metrics.incr('somekey', 1, tags=['key:val', 'key2:val'])
```

That format for tags is weird if we think of tags as always having a key and a value. Datadog doesn't require that, though.

The datadog backend has restrictions on keys and values:

> Tags must start with a letter, and after that may contain alphanumerics, underscores, minuses, colons, periods and slashes. Other characters will get converted to underscores. Tags can be up to 200 characters long and support unicode. Tags will be converted to lowercase.
> 
> For optimal functionality, we recommend constructing tags that use the key:value syntax. Examples of commonly used metric tag keys are env, instance, name, and role. Note that device, host, and source are "reserved" tag keys and cannot be specified in the standard way.
> 
> We store one time series per host + metric + tag combination on our backend, thus we cannot support infinitely bounded tags. Please don't include endlessly growing tags in your metrics, like timestamps or user ids. Please limit each metric to 1000 tags.
> (https://help.datadoghq.com/hc/en-us/articles/204312749-Getting-started-with-tags)

First, we should fix the Markus docs regarding tags to make those restrictions clearer.

After that, seems prudent to clean up tags somewhere.

Maybe Markus should clean up tags before sending them to backends? Then all backends get the same tags.

Maybe the backends themselves should clean up tags if they need to? Then we don't clean up tags that don't need cleaning up.

Maybe we do a bit of both?

Maybe we do a utility function that cleans up tags and takes either a list or a dict and returns a list of tags?

Hi, first of all bonsai is mostly written in C and uses libldap2 on Unix platforms and WinLDAP on Microsoft. ldap3 is all pure python, so it's inherently slower.
Also the "get_info=ALL" parameter of th Server connections means that when you open the connection the whole schema and the whole server info are read from the server (this is requested by the LDAP v3 protocol specification). I don't think that the bonsai library do the same. 
Also, the "ASYNC" strategy is a bit slower because when instances it creates a new python thread and doens't use an async loop.

Lastly in your code you're summing up the bonsai test time to the ldap3 time so the ldap3 result includes also the bonsai time!

I dont' have bonsai installed but I get the following result for ldap3 in my lab:

all info, ASYNC strategy: 1.7 seconds
no info, ASYNC strategy: 0.21 seconds (get_info=NONE in server object, check_names=None in connection object)
no info, SYNC strategy: 0.03 seconds

This is probaly still slower than a C search but I think it's quite fast in a pure python library.

```

def test_ldap3():

                    )
        # print(conn.get_response(id_mess))


start_ldap3 = time()
test_ldap3()
stop_ldap3 = time() - start_ldap3
```

>  How are you going to have multiple threads running it?

When GIL is released and dlib computation is running in several threads, they all running simultaneously  on several cores.

When GIL is acquired only one of this threads can be running at this time and all others are waiting for it.

```
thread_1 = Thread(target=dlib_computation)
thread_2 = Thread(target=dlib_computation)

thread_1.start()
thread_2.start()
```

```
GIL acquired:

<=== thread 1 ===>                    <some Python code> <=== thread 1 ===>
                   <=== thread 2 ===>                                       <=== thread 2 ===>
```

```
GIL released:

<=== thread 1 ===> <some Python code> <=== thread 1 ===>
<=== thread 2 ===>                    <=== thread 2 ===>
```

Yes. this is by design. If no `exp` claim is encoded in the token then this token is meant to have an infinite lifetime. RFC 7519 says 

>  Use of this claim is OPTIONAL.

(https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.4)

Hello,

Good call. The signal in question has a long name (41 characters). As such, `signalName` and `self['CNBlock'][dg][cg][channel]['signalName']` are not the same, since the first contains the long name (from line 209), and the latter has the short name. In line 221 you are checking for what basically is the short name in `self['ChannelNamesByDG'][dg]`, but with line 230 you effectively put the long name in that list. As such, the check on line 221 you will always return `False`. Line 224 will pick up the duplicate, will append the dg number to it, and move on. This will catch situations with two duplicates, but not situations with more than two duplicates, such as in my case.

So, basically, this is the order of events, keeping in mind that `self['CNBlock'][dg][cg][channel]['signalName']` contains the short name, and `signalName` contains the long name:

- the check on line 221 returns `False`
- the check on line 224 returns `False`
- line 228 replaces the short name with the long name
- lines 229 and 230 save the long name in `self['ChannelNamesByDG'][dg]` and in `self['allChannelList']`
- for the next label, line 221 returns `False`, because that list contains the long name
- line 224 returns `True`, because we're checking the long name, and the `dg` number is appended
- lines 229 and 230 save the long name with the appended `dg` to the lists

The last 3 lines repeat for every other occurrence, leading to multiple channels with the same name, with the `dg` attached.

Seems like the solution is to use `signalName` instead of `self['CNBlock'][dg][cg][channel]['signalName']`

TreeCorr's estimate of varxi is usually an underestimate, since it does not (cannot) include the sample variance component of varxi.  So I strongly recommend not trusting it for anything important.  Instead, people normally use either jackknife estimates (if you want a variance from the data) or theory/simulation covariance matrices.

But to answer your specific question, the formula for varxi in each bin for NN is simply:
[(weight in RR for this bin) * (total weight in all DD bins) / (total weight in all RR bins)]**-1

And shape noise is irrelevant for NN.  That only shows up for correlation functions involving G (shear).

Actually the example is wrong here. Parsing the arguments in the utils.py module's global scope is the problem which causes this behaviour. To improve the example, just move the parsing to local scope, e.g. a function:

main.py
``` python
import configargparse
import utils

p = configargparse.getArgumentParser()
p.add_argument("-x", help="Main module setting")
p.add_argument("--m-setting", help="Main module setting")
options = p.parse_known_args()   # using p.parse_args() here may raise errors.

print("from main.py:", options)
utils.main()
```

utils.py
``` python
import configargparse
p = configargparse.getArgumentParser()
p.add_argument("--utils-setting", help="Config-file-settable option for utils")

def main():
    options = p.parse_known_args()
    # do something with the options
    print("from utils.py:", options)
```
Then `python main.py --help` produces the output you expected and `python main.py -x anything` produces
```
$ python main.py -x anything
from main.py: (Namespace(m_setting=None, utils_setting=None, x='anything'), [])
from utils.py: (Namespace(m_setting=None, utils_setting=None, x='anything'), [])
```

By default, interactive usage is switched on when importing gtk:
http://www.pygtk.org/pygtk2reference/gtk-functions.html#function-gtk--set-interactive

Calling **gtk.set_interactive(0)** disables interactive use of PyGTK.  So agreed, we don't have a reason to need interactive usage.

Having PyGTK in interactive mode (the default) causes problems when a background thread attempts to print to stdout while Python is attempting to get input from stdin.  Since ``pyperclip`` isn't interactively using PyGTK, we are much safer explicitly disabling interactive mode.

`print(pyperclip.paste(primary=True))`

I believe I made an assumption that `#S` control lines always have a blank line preceding.  This file clearly does not obey that imagined rule.  The scan starts with the `#S` line, everything before that is from activities before the scan macro was called.  Usually, this is from a previous scan but also may be triggered by command line actions.  The `qdo` most often registers data in data files that is unexpected for a scan.

BUT, this file was written by custom macros that operate a CCD camera and have generated output that labels itself as part of the upcoming scan but appears *before* the `#S` line.

I don't see how to second-guess this content in a general way.  The `SPEC_Scan` plugin has this documentation:

    In SPEC data files, the ``#S`` control line indicates the 
    start of a *scan* block.

Correct, localhost was still defined in /etc/hosts for both IPv4 and IPv6, after removing the /etc/hosts entries Electrumx resolves localhost as its IPv4 address 127.0.0.1 only.

From:

```
127.0.0.1	localhost

# The following lines are desirable for IPv6 capable hosts
::1     localhost ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
```

To:

```
127.0.0.1	localhost

# The following lines are desirable for IPv6 capable hosts
# ::1     localhost ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
```

When both IPv4 and IPv6 entries are defined in /etc/hosts, Python resolves localhost as "::1", while in fact it should resolve it as both "127.0.0.1" and "::1" and it should attempt to create a RPC listening port for both interfaces. What we observe here is that when localhost has an IPv6 defined it will try to open a listening port for ::1, but will fail (because there is no IPv6 interface on the system) and will not attempt to create a listening port for 127.0.0.1 (while it should!).

Are you sure this is a libc or Python issue?

For reference, this is how IPv6 is disabled (https://wiki.debian.org/DebianIPv6):

In /etc/sysctl.conf:

```
net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6 = 1
net.ipv6.conf.default.disable_ipv6 = 1
net.ipv6.conf.lo.disable_ipv6 = 1
net.ipv6.conf.eth0.disable_ipv6 = 1
```

Regarding same/different child ids for different measurements, that's not related to pymysensors, but to home assistant. It's got to do with how we generate entity_ids and how home assistant in general works.

Each entity should only have one main state value, eg a sensor measurement. An entity can have multiple supplemental state attributes on top of that but only ever one state at the same time. Since each of the sensor measurements maps to a main state in home assistant, each sensor measurement should create a new entity. Each entity requires a unique entity_id. For a long time the mysensors entity_id structure in home assistant has been [sketchname]_[node_id]_[child_id].

So currently without changing the entity_id structure it's not good to have the same child id for different measurements, if you want to avoid problems with changing entity_ids.

Also the base concept of the mysensors implementation in home assistant is built around 1 child is 1 entity per platform.

There's no limiting reason that I can see in the pulse meter example sketch that would hinder you from using different child ids for the different measurements.

That said even mapping a Python timedelta to an ISO duration b/c you can once again lose information if it includes the starting date. Bottom line is the mapping of AMAZON.DATE (and possibly AMAZON.DURATION) types to Python date/time/delta types is not a 1 to 1 match.

Interesting question. Thinking about it for a bit i believe the best bet here would actually be to do the authentication request synchronously and just use the thread that was allocated to work on the original request which will already be in the background. 

If you're doing lots of requests that may or may not need individual auth processes that should be fine, on the other hand if you're doing many async requests that would need to auth together, e.g. many calls to an API when your token expires, you'd probably need to do something more involved/advanced. It'd probably be out of scope for `requests_futures` to try and first-class support that pattern, but it would be a interesting one to solve. I think I'd probably separate the auth process into it's own thing and come up with a way to catch auth failures and just retry requests that didn't work due to auth problems. I don't think I'd try and auth individual requests when they need it since that would result in many authentication attempts in parallel.

The original issue was about reading the m bounding box for shapes when there was none to be found. After some digging this seems to have been caused by a bug where PyShp used the file header m range to indicate whether it should read m values from shapes. But the code misinterpreted the ESRI spec on the difference between the meaning of 0.0 (can be valid, but also can indicate non-m shapetype) and -10e38 (if all m values in the file were set to nodata). In addition, using the m range values to decide whether to read shape m values is a bit misleading, since a file can be written with faulty m range values, and this should rather be determined directly from the shapetype. 

Changed the code behavior on this in 4f89bebd89802e044e5d92c9e3272f20cfc7d1df.

The reason this is happening is because the undecorated class `C` does not have a `__init__`, but the decorated one does. There are no fields defined in the decorated class, so nothing gets initialized. The generated `__init__` cannot guess what parameters are needed in the base class `__init__`, so it never tries to call it. The mechanisms exist to make this work (either `@dataclass(init=False)` or `__post_init__`), but you do have to understand what's happening.

import time, sys
from jsonrpclib.SimpleJSONRPCServer import SimpleJSONRPCServer


class Control:
    def __init__(self):
        self.count = 0
    def SetBPState(self, name, value):
        print "SetBPState",name,value
    
    def GetVOState(self, name):
        print "GetVoState",name
        self.count = self.count+1
        return self.count 
        


class Server:
    def __init__(self, ip, port):
        self.continueloop = True
        self.daemon = None
        self.ip = ip
        self.port = port
        self.ctrl = None
        
    def Loop(self):
        while self.continueloop:
            self.Start()
        
    def Restart(self):
        self.Stop()
    
    def Quit(self):
        self.continueloop = False
        self.Stop()
    
    def Start(self):
        if True:
            self.daemon = SimpleJSONRPCServer((self.ip,self.port))
            self.ctrl = Control()

            # on enregistre les fonctions disponibles via JSON-RPC            
            self.daemon.register_function(self.ctrl.SetBPState,'SetBPState')
            self.daemon.register_function(self.ctrl.GetVOState,'GetVOState')

        
            print "The daemon runs on port :",self.port
            print "The object's uri is : %s" % (self.ip)
            sys.stdout.flush()
            
            self.daemon.serve_forever()

#        except KeyboardInterrupt:
#            self.Quit()
        else:
            print "sleep"
            time.sleep(0.5)
    
    def Stop(self):
        self.daemon.shutdown(True)
        
        
pserver = Server("localhost", 3000)
pserver.Loop()

```

regarding 1)
redis-rdb-tools has a tool that converts the RDB file into a stream of redis commands. if you send these commands to a redis server via a client connection, you effectively load that RDB into the target redis (merging it with whatever is already there, unless you FLUSHALL in advance)

regarding 2)
What i mean is that if you're already connected to redis as a client, you can download an RDB file directly form redis (without doing BGSAVE and any ssh/scp).

You do this by sending SYNC command (like slaves do), download the RDB file that you get a response and either save it, or better yet, feed the stream of bytes directly into redis-rdb-tools and avoid the slowdown of writing and reading from the local disk.

Please note that you can send REPLCONF CAPA EOF, and if the master is set with repl-diskless-sync then the master sends you an RDB file without storing it on the master's disk, but note that the response to the SYNC command is slightly different in this case since the master doesn't know the size of the RDB file ahead of time.

You're looking to change the CSV output to have `;` as the delimeter, yes? That would be controlled in the renderer rather than the parser.

To create a renderer that uses semicolons, you could do something like:

```python
class MyCustomRenderer (CSVRenderer):
    writer_opts = dict(
        delimiter=';'
    )
```

Then you could use that renderer on a per-view basis, or set it in the `DEFAULT_RENDERER_CLASSES` setting.

I don't know what you mean by "I am an outside user".

Only one user is authenticating here: the service account for `enterprise_id='xxxxxx'`. That service account is, by definition, within `enterprise_id='xxxxxx'`.

But that service account is, in most respects, a normal user. It can only access content that it owns, or content that it is collaborated on. It can't see all content within the enterprise.

If you really want to be able to upload a file into a folder, it needs to own or be collaborated on that folder.

If you have more questions or problems with service accounts, please post a question on community.box.com , unless there is a problem specific to the Python SDK.

I understand that this seems confusing.  Here's what's happening:

*Watches* are cancelled when the root is removed, which updates your *subscription* to that watch to let you know that you will never get another update; the thing that was anchoring the system resource has been removed so there's nothing more we can do about it.

Because a relative_root removal doesn't cancel a watch, and because we're still able to watch the containing directory of the relative root and can tell you if/when that directory comes back, we don't send a cancellation notice in that case.

Watches are the unit of observation, whereas subscriptions are filters on the change stream.

So, we're not going to be able to send a cancel notice for the `relative_root` rename/delete, but you can detect the removal of the directory if your subscription expression doesn't filter that out.

I'm wary of changing this behavior: I think we may have code in production that relies on the subscriptions remaining across delete and recreates like this, and if we added implicit subscription deletion by default, we'd break some expectations.   I'm open to the idea of adding a flag to the `subscribe` command to opt-in to this behavior.

The network doesn't know what characters are, it just learns the probabilities of pieces of audio matching one of N different classes. In our case, the number N of classes happens to be 29 (`n_character` in the code), which is the 26 letters of the English alphabet plus three indices reserved for space, apostrophe and the CTC blank label (although we currently strip apostrophes from the source texts). If your alphabet has more letters, all you have to do is increase the number of classes accordingly, and possibly update text.py to map the Unicode characters to indices between 0 and `n_character`. Then no special pre and post-processing of the text is needed, and the network will learn your alphabet "natively".

Are you saying to do the completeness check early in `TaskProcess.run` and return immediately if the task is already complete? If so, I think we would also need to report the complete state to scheduler to guarantee that downstream task(s) would be handed out in get_work. The central scheduler won't behave correctly when the task __init__ method is overwritten. That was the issue that I was running into earlier when testing.
The following changes worked for me:
`def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):` -> `def initialize_k8s_job(self):`
take out the first line calling super.
and then just put `self.initialize_k8s_job()` at the beginning of the `run` method.

You could accomplish that a different way, but that will allow it to run with local scheduler or with the central scheduler.

Sorry, just catching up on this now.  To summarize, it sounds like we're saying:

For any given precedence level (i.e config file, env vars, cmdline args, etc), the akid/sak will always trump a profile value if all three are specified at the same precedence value.

A consequence of this is that in order to get a "profile" to take, you have to specify it at the next precedence level (or higher) of whatever is explicitly specifying the akid/skid.  So if akid/sak are in env vars, profile has to be as a cmd line option or in boto3 via a method arg.

In which case, I think that makes sense.  Thanks for looking into this.

It is indeed valid to release under both, but only under the following conditions:

- A project was previously - and correctly! - released under MIT. fuzzywuzzy never was, as we included `StringMatcher.py` in the source out of the gate.
- Derivatives must use a compatible license. MIT is not a compatible license with GPLv2, at least in the way you are relicensing.
- Only the copyright holder - SeatGeek - can relicense. You are not the copyright holder, so you cannot relicense.
- The copyright holder must get permission from all contributors in order to relicense. We have the ability to relicense all contributions from SeatGeek employees, but would have to reach out to all other contributors and confirm that their changes are okay to be relicensed.

Taking another stab at this issue, I have some thoughts, which ramble a bit, so I've broken it down into three segments.

# The Two-Package Solution

I have another idea, almost a proposal. What if setuptools provided two packages, the current, natural one as is currently in place, and another, perhaps named `setuptools_built` or `setuptools_bundle` or `setuptools_all`, which is essentially a pre-built, dependency-free package (perhaps just setuptools with its dependencies vendored). This special package would be "build dependency" of setuptools itself and any of its dependencies. As a build-only dependency, installers would be advised only to install this package in a transient manner for the duration of the build step, enabling packages like `appdirs` and `requests` and `pyparsing` to rely on setuptools for their builds (as they did before). This package, much like Setuptools 33 and earlier, would be buildable and installable with distutils only from source.

This approach is potentially messy as it relies on installers not to install this package permanently, and might create weird behaviors that might prove difficult to troubleshoot (i.e. which setuptools is present when an error occurred). It does, however, nicely solve the bootstrapping problem (providing a bootstrapping package) and also elegantly solves the "which setuptools is required to install a given setuptools" problem, as setuptools would declare its own build dependency, allowing setuptools to eschew much of the logic in `setuptools.sandbox` which was created to support the self-install model.

To some extent, this idea is dependent on the implementation of PEP 518 and would required build system like Spack and system packages to honor that spec when building packages. That feels like a fairly small investment if it ultimately means that setuptools (and any other build tool) can declare its dependencies naturally, allow itself and its dependencies to depend on it (or other build tools), but can also own the responsibility for providing bootstrapping.

# Build Systems Should Special-Case Build Dependency Resolution

Alternatively, these build systems could be adapted to have a special handling for build-time dependencies, such that if a package has a build-time dependency on setuptools (or other build tools), the resolution isn't to build/install those packages, but instead has a distinct mechanism for providing those packages. This approach would allow each build tools to have control over how these bootstrapping issues are addressed. Some might just vendor the known build dependencies (namely setuptools today, but sure to include others). Others might do some clever trick to resolve the dependency tree and put the sources on sys.path while building the build dependencies.

# PyPA Provides Build Dependency Resolver

As I think about it more, perhaps this clever trick is exactly what we should develop - a tool for building and installing (transiently or permanently) a set of packages that are mutually dependent on each other. This tool would have special handling for working with source packages and making them available without installing them.

For the following example, I'll call this tool "source-resolver".

So for example, a system package builder wants to build 'appdirs'. 'appdirs' declares it has a build-time dependency on "setuptools" (any version), so it wraps the build step in call to `source_resolver('setuptools')`. That routine will retrieve the setuptools source, extract it to a temporary directory, add it to sys.path, and then recurse on its dependencies. It will do this in a context and undo its changes at the close of the context. In this context, the builder invokes the build steps on appdirs, producing the package (and possibly installing it).

`source_resolver`, like pip, could take an argument of a local directory where the sources are already present.

This approach would require that all build dependencies are importable from source without an intermediate build or install step. Or alternatively, the spec could be expanded to allow packages to declare which of their build dependencies require this special handling. Or maybe this routine is used as a fallback when a build fails using the normal process.

----

There's a lot going on here, so I'm going to break for now and let these thoughts continue to simmer.

What would happen if we said "because Klein & Werkzeug differ in weltanschauung, declaring a route to be both `strict_slashes=True` and `branch=True` is verboten"? This could take the shape of throwing an exception if both are truthy, of ignoring one if the other is set, or just emitting a warning that users may be setting themselves up for counterintuitive behavior. I'm not 100% sure that that would resolve the inconsistency, but I feel obliged to mention that sometimes _removing_ features is the right answer, so I want to gesture in that direction.

I do think, however, that saying "a route returning None is synonymous with setting the response code to `500`" is not best; it feels like that _is_ guessing. The default response in `t.w.h.Request` is 200 and the default return value of a function is `None`, so if you the framework user don't act to change those values, you get the defaults. The "that's the default, change it if you want different behavior," though, assumes that a user knows both of those facts off the top of their head—and the Twisted one is not expressed with magnificent clarity by the docs, while the Python one is broadly-but-not-universally known (my personal context: just last week I unthinkingly dropped into Lisp mode and assumed that the return value of the innermost function call would just bubble up; I got `None` instead and had a _maddening_ bug for an hour or so).

After the Monday 2/2 developers' forum we agreed on the following as far as the 
new indexer and the old indexer refactoring are concerned:                         
                                                                                   
1. The existing table field_tag that correlates MARC tags with logical fields
will be deprecated and instead for this correlation the legacy decorator in
JSONAlchemy will be used.                                                          
                                                                                   
2. The ability to include multiple logical fields in an index will be deprecated
and this functionality will be offered through virtual indeces.                    
                                                                                   
Based on these changes the new JSONAlchemy configuration could be as follows:   
                                                                                   
```                                                                                
foobar_field:                                                                      
    @legacy(. . .)                                                              
    # Other configuration                                                          
   indexer:                                                                        
        native:{                                                                   
            # Native indexer configurations                                        
            tokenizer: 'BibIndexJournalTokenizer',                                 
            option_y: 'value_y',                                                   
            # execute this functions in cascade before indexed the field           
            pre_execute: 'function1', 'function2',                                 
            # execute this functions in cascade after indexed the field            
            post_execute: 'function1', 'function2',                                
            # Other native configuration might be needed                        
        }                                                                          
        elasticsearch:{                                                            
            # Elasticsearch configurations (At least elasticsearch or native       
            # should be present)                                                   
            analyzer: 'my_custom_analyzer'                                         
        }                                                                          
        virtal_index:                                                              
            # A list with names of the virtual indexes this index is part of       
            ['index1', "index2"]                                                   
```

Right now I force only the first type, basically copying the "produces", "consumes" behavior from swagger 2.
The feature you suggest raises several interesting questions like:
1. Should there be a handler function per response content type? If not, do we know how to serialize the result of function into every conceivable content type? What's the implementation of serializing the response to text/plan, application/xml?
2. Which content types should be supported for both serialization and validation? Could we just extend the validator_map to include content type, and require the user to provide a validator? Could we copy this pattern for serialization?
2. Which layer should handle the Accept header? Does it make it to the handler function? Does the handler function need to return the correct data based on the Accept header?
3. What about Accept header behavior like q-factor weighting?

Right now, IIRC the behavior is:
- if it's json, serialize the object to json and validate it
- if it's something else, attach the correct header but assume it is serialized and bypass validation

It would be kind of cool from a user perspective to be able to just provide a decorator interface that the user could subclass.

@my_xml_serializer

> I know this "Delete removes lock" is stated all over the internet, but what it actually means is
> "delete removes the lock and then deletes the file".

Not exactly. It is done atomically at the FS level. What it means - nothing can read or write or lock the file until the bulk transaction is finished.

In your way there is short short time between calls. So say run your program with cpulimit -l 1 for example (this will limit it to 1% of available CPU and it will be really slow) and if you unlock the file and then another process comes to terms to lock it within the timeframe between calls to unlock and to delete means your app will allow the second app to run.

The probability is low but it is there and if it happens - it would be horrible to debug because your code advertises as a locking code

The only correct one which works atomically in all modern operating systems is https://github.com/dirk-attraktor/Singleton-Test/blob/master/SingletonOsremove.py .

However to atomically lock after creation you need to os.open it with the flags to lock before and not just create the file and lock it (same race condition).

Hi,

Thank you for your time for the writeup. I agree with you. With the presence of path based routing, the SDK doesn't have enough information to do the grouping correctly. In your example it is very likely that the `resource/*` is served by a single service. But there are also counterexamples:  `data/1` is routed to control-plane fleet while `data/2` is routed to data-plane fleet.

I think it really depends on who owns those services. If you own those services that serving `resource/*` and they are also integrated with X-Ray, they will also actively emit segments. The X-Ray service graph will respect the segment emitted from the server side other than caller. So if all `resource/1`, `resource2` are served with a Django app which emits segment named `my_service`, then in service graph you will only see a node called `my_service`.

If you don't own the service that serving those requests, then the question comes down to if you want to aggregate them or not and aggregate up to which level on the url schema. Generally I would recommend to not aggregate anything because you don't know things behind the scene and it can be difficult to identify issues if all urls are aggregated. But I definitely would like to know more about your use case here. The SDK will for sure provide better customer experience by having more flexibility on user configuration.

Please let me know your thoughts.

@tamcy: Are you working with a name- or CID-keyed source font?

Keep in mind that these AFDKO tools operate only on CFFs, CIDFont resources, and name-keyed Type 1 fonts, and if you specify an OpenType font (aka 'sfnt' resource), the output will not be an OpenType font, but rather one of the formats listed previously.

With that said, _mergeFonts_ is the ideal tool for merging glyphs from multiple fonts, and as part of this process it can exclude a glyph from one source font so that the same glyph (same name of CID) in another source font, such as a corrected version, will be used in the merge (aka destination) font.

If you simply want to exclude a glyph, use the _tx_ tool and its _-gx_ command-line option, with the name of the glyph, or its CID if it's a CID-keyed font, as its argument.

I went poking around a bunch, and I think I'm going to label this garbage-in-garbage-out, because table inheritance doesn't automatically trigger this problem—inheriting another table's Meta may well trigger it, but that's basically indistinguishable from supplying a bad column name to ``hidden_columns``, which I definitely don't want to silence.

If there's no inheritance going on, then the problem must be coming from a manipulation of the table's column options.  With the newer Datatable class, columns should be easily manipulated after the object is constructed (``del the_datatable.columns['foo']``).

In Legacy tables, which I suspect triggered this, the columns must have been manipulated in the ``get_datatable_options()`` step, but the ``hidden_columns`` setting wasn't updated to match the new runtime column spec.  I'm going to treat that as a misconfigured table since it's the same as declaring a table with a bad name in ``hidden_columns``.

Relating to the sortable columns, the same logic applies, I haven't been handling it there because the ``normalize_config_ordering()`` is the only place it's referenced, and it's only used in relation to column names we know are good (no KeyError).

If there is a scenario that trips an ``unsortable_columns`` KeyError, I'll have to deal with that separately, but as of now I'm not sure it's a concern.

As I mentioned above, I think the challenge here is that we have competing design goals and different solutions are better at satisfying different goals.

On CLI args:

Moses takes the approach of "everything is a factor", with surface forms as the first factor.  If we're taking the approach of "factors are metadata" then there is a case for having separate explicit CLI args for them.  Based on the discussion, this design sounds fine to me if we use `--source-factors` and `--source-factors-num-embed` for consistency.

On formats:

I agree that whatever format we use should be parsed into data structures by an input filter and not require further modifications to inference code.  I'll make a case for Moses-style token-level factored input like `An|O exam@@|B ple|I`:
- If we're already using a special paste function that strips existing tabs, we'll need to run a script anyway.  We could just include a factor-paste script to use instead.  This can also be our first line of defense for catching non-token-parallel source files.
- The widely-used Moses tokenizer already escapes pipes and other special characters by default so people wouldn't need to manually add a special case.  We would also only run the input filter if the factored input flag is present (either as a CLI arg or in the model config).  This is double protection against factor delimiters causing problems.
- Token-level factors with a non-whitespace delimiter are much easier for humans to read and debug.  They can also be fed through utility scripts like corpus cleaning.

If we have filters for both separate files and inline factors, we could give users a choice.  If I were building a research system, I would prefer a single input file with all factors that I could run utility and analysis scripts on.  Someone building a giant system might prefer not to take up twice the disk space.

I would prefer to include the factors in the input file (at least for the CLI), i.e. the "An|O exam@@|B ple|I" proposed by Michael. I think this is less error prone. There should be a flag to specify the use of factors and an option to define the separator. The parsing of the input lines can be left out of the API and be done in the translate tool.

This behavior is a consequence of the way we chose some "convenience" API methods. If you include the code you use I can point out more specifically, but essentially the `.text` property for the cell has to convert a potentially complex XML hierarchy into a single string that's useful for general purposes.

If you want to get your text more exactly, you'll need to do that conversion yourself by looping through the paragraphs in the cell and inserting them as paragraphs in the new cell. So something roughly like:

```python
for paragraph in cell.paragraphs:
    ... copy paragraph contents to new table ...
```

If you want to preserve font formatting, you'll need to go down to the run level.

Another possible option is to use deepcopy() to copy the XML in the cell and write it into the new cell. This is a bit more brittle theoretically, but might turn out to be easier for your case. You'll need some understanding of how python-docx uses lxml to manipulate the underlying XML to make this work.

the **_TRICK_** is that with our use case (which i understand you deem unusual) is switching users. our initial ingestion and hourly updates are all done by one app: first creating and adding attributes, and then updating attributes and tags as needed as more info is aquired in a future hourly feed. hence, the **_TRICK_** is as follows (was confusing to me that i could `tag/untag` an attribute in an event created by a user in `org`, but to update the comment in that same attribute i need to be a user in `orgc`):

- for the initial `event_add` and subsequent `update_event` that have `add_attribute` and `tag/untag`, the app talks to PyMISP as a user in `org`.
- however, note that `add_event` creates with differing `org` (set to our internal analyst org name, i.e. "we" own this event even though it comes from a feed) and `orgc` (set to represent the feed creater, i.e. not "us").
- the _**TRICK**_ is then when there's a need to call `update_attribute` (which is via the `uuid` of the attribute, and not changed as part of a call to `update_event`) to revise a comment in a future feed update to an existing attr in an existing event that same single app needs to use a PyMISP connection for user in `orgc` (and not the usual one in `org`).

import docker

`link_df()` does not assume that the data are in chronological order. It is a side effect of using Pandas that the most general way to do it is also the easiest to code, and that for most users it happens fast enough that they never notice.

I'm now seriously thinking that `link_df_iter()` is going to fix this for @mdgratale . `groupby` is super efficient and runs only once. If you use `pandas.concat(..., ignore_index=True).reset_index(inplace=True, drop=True)` to handle the output, you'll get a big array in memory in a fraction of the time.

The "bug fix" for `link_df()` would be to (optionally) assume that the DataFrame was sorted by frame number, and then use lower-level methods to read and write. That would keep everything to a constant time per frame. Or just make `link_df()` a wrapper around `link_df_iter()`, and get rid of the ability to link a DataFrame in-place.

Dear @pipermerriam ,


Thank you for your message. My responses follow.

1. I sincerely apologize for not explaining the motivations behind the way in which PyWebAssembly was written. PyWebAssembly follows the Wasm Spec document closely, referencing where each function was defined. This is useful for the following reasons.
   i) The Spec document can be used as a user's manual.
   ii) PyWebAssembly can be audited alongside the Spec document.
   iii) When Wasm 1.1 is released, PyWebAssembly can be updated alongside the Spec document.
   iv) PyWebAssembly does not introduce invariants or design decisions that are not in the Spec document. There are many subtleties in the 150 page Spec document, and invariants may be difficult to maintain, as I have learned.
   v) Implementing the Spec document has allowed me to find errors and submit fixes to the Spec document, and I have more fixes coming.

PyWebAssembly is also structured for my personal economy-of-thought as it is being developed and studied for errors. I do not know any better than to follow the Spec document, but I have a lot to learn, and I am eager to improve myself.

In any case, PyWebAssembly was brought to your attention because it is 100% finished for your purposes, modulo tracing which is to-be-done, and tests which are not applicable to your application. This is not to speak of aesthetics.

2. Tracing Wasm execution is possible at the granularity of opcodes. When testing, I step through execution of each opcode, printing the operand stack and other info. I am open to your suggestions on how to implement tracing in a way which meets your parameters and specifications.

3. Lane is skilled at quickly gluing code together. Full integration may be complex because Wasm has different structure and properties than EVM, and it may be difficult to honor the invariants and designs which you have specified for EVM. Because of this complexity and resulting difficulty, I am willing to offer assistance in your project, although I do not wish to hinder your progress, nor do I wish to create any feelings of obligation.

I wish you only the best success.


Sincerely,

The most recent suggestion was that the namespace for core jupyter tags would in theory be the null string with no colons. The goal is not to make other package namespaces inaccessible (that would be basically impossible), but rather to make their use as explicit as possible. 

I know that we could modify the code to respect the convention that was proposed, but it's dangerous to have magic values cover so much area in the space of potential strings. I'd much prefer if we're going to allow both of those in, that we restrict it to the exact two strings that need to be covered to maintain the current use-case.

More generally, though, my point was that we need to come to a consensus on this **now** before 5.1 is released. Once 5.1 is released, we have to live with that decision for backwards compatibility reasons. 

As you can see it's already _really_ complicated; I fear it will only get more complicated if we aren't pretty strict about enforcing this as soon as possible. 

And regarding duplication… as long as it's a thing we need to do once and never again I think we can probably avoid this problem by switching to conventions and making some way to overwrite the code that you just wrote. The hard part is that nbval would then need to also be a notebook_extension… which those devs might not want to do. 

It's worth noting that for switching conventions, it wouldn't be hard to write a preprocessor in nbconvert that would automatically convert metadata from one convention to another (something like this in preprocess.cell).

```python 
old_conv=re.compile(r"nbval-(.*)")
new_conv= "nbval:"
cell.metadata.tags = [new_conv +  old_conv.match(tag).group(0) if old_conv.match(tag) else tag for tag in cell.metadata.tags ]
```

…which you could apply once to all the notebooks in your project and be done with it.

Similarly you could write a custom save_hook that would apply a similar sort of transform so that you wouldn't need to actually _type_ redundant tags even if they needed to be _stored_ redundantly in order to handle separation of concerns appropriately.

I think that 'OSCAR_MAIN_TEMPLATE_DIR' should be removed alltogether, there is no reason to require users to manually specify the template path of an application, since ``django.template.loaders.app_directories.Loader`` is installed by default. All that need to be done to allow ``django.template.loaders.app_directories.Loader`` to find the oscar templates, is add 'oscar' to the end of the list ``get_core_apps`` returns.

Obviously, the template paths should be fixed everywhere to become 'oscar/dashboard/file.html' instead of 'dashboard/file.html' that would nicely namespace the templates adding robustness against conflicts.

For the mechanism for allowing same template inheritance, I would just reverse the mechanism. Make users add 'templates/oscar' to the template path for their secondary oscar template path. That way they can extend oscar/base.html like this:
```
# inside project/templates/oscar/dashboard/file.html
    {% extends 'dashboard/file.html' %}  # secondary template path has no
```
That kind of reintroduces the conflict possibility, but this time it is inside the user's project, and he can always descide to include dirname(dirname('templates/oscar') to the filesystem  searchpath allowing him to do same template inheritance as described above.

So after removing the need for manual configuration of oscar templates, and removing the need for 'OSCAR_MAIN_TEMPLATE_DIR' we should add a 'OSCAR_SECONDARY_TEMPLATE_DIR' var that users can use to implement same template inheritance. It would have exactly the same value as the 'OSCAR_MAIN_TEMPLATE_DIR' because we reversed the main and secundary template dirs.

To remain backwards compatible, OSCAR_MAIN_TEMPLATE_DIR should be deprecated, with a warning.  Explaining users should move their templates inside a folder named 'oscar' to make sure they can be found in the future. Each view could then look for the template in the secondary folder before looking in the main folder, but only if OSCAR_MAIN_TEMPLATE_DIR is still present in the DIRS setting. Somewhat similar how the django admin looks for templates in different locations.

Hm, an interesting thing; I implemented the test which requires that `input_to_hidden.output_shape[0] is None` or `input_to_hidden.output_shape[0] == n_batch*n_seq ` when `precompute_input = True`, and it causes a bunch of the tests to fail.  It's arguably overly strict; many Lasagne layers happily ignore when you tell them you're going to give them one batch size and you give them another (as they should):

```
In [1]: import lasagne
In [2]: l_in = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((10, 20))
In [3]: l_dense = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(l_in, 30)
In [4]: out = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_dense)
In [5]: import theano
In [6]: import numpy as np
In [7]: out.eval({l_in.input_var: np.zeros((10, 20), dtype=theano.config.floatX)}).shape
Out[7]: (10, 30)
In [8]: out.eval({l_in.input_var: np.zeros((100, 20), dtype=theano.config.floatX)}).shape
Out[8]: (100, 30)
```

So, I guess the question is wether to be overly strict and throw errors when we don't necessarily always need to, or just to be careful about documentation so that when a layer is used (e.g. Conv2DLayer with a certain backend) which doesn't allow the batch size to change, the user can understand why the error that comes up is coming up.  Any thoughts?

> Clipping the predictions to [1e-10,1-1e-10] works, but was wondering if this should not be built in?

This is definitely something we should leave up to the researcher, we cannot clip anything by default. We could of course add a `clip` keyword to `binary_crossentropy`, but `binary_crossentropy(preds, targets, clip=1e-10)` doesn't read any easier than `binary_crossentropy(T.clip(preds, -1e-10, 1e-10), targets)`, so it would just complicate our code. Besides, there are many possible reasons and many possible solutions for NaNs: You might also be successful by lowering your learning rate, changing the weight initialization, normalizing/scaling your inputs, adding/changing regularization terms... or maybe Theano misses some numerical stabilization optimization, [as for the categorical crossentropy with one-hot targets](https://github.com/Theano/Theano/issues/3162).

Strongly in favor of this. I've been doing a bit of thinking on this, talking to @ewmassey and @adparker. I'll describe this on a components level (per project / concept), then we can tie it all together. ## Commuter API Endpoint Publishing a new notebook (or an old one for that matter) as a new published entity: ``` POST /api/v1/published/ {notebook content, could be similar to the contents API} ``` Response: ``` { id: "the-id-of-the-published-document", version: "1", # This could be a hash of the notebook too url: "", # Path to the notebook, for linking out to } ``` Publishing a new version of a notebook ``` PUT /api/v1/published/123 {notebook content} ``` Response is similar to the POST ## Notebook metadata A published notebook updates metadata under `.metadata.papermill.publish` ```js { cells: [...], metadata: { papermill: { publication: { id: "1234", endpoint: "https://mycommuter" } } } } ``` This captures the endpoint so that publishing again will update the published copy, allowing an author to iterate and publish again. ## Clients - nteract, papermill nteract will include a button to publish, including a way to set up credentials papermill will have a `publish` command like so: ``` papermill publish myNotebook.ipynb ``` They both modify the notebook in place to include the above mentioned metadata. ## Versioning In terms of UI on commuter, I want people to be able to: * Link to a published notebook * Link to a specific version of a published notebook (by hash or version number) * Publish from the commuter browsing interface straight to published ## Opinions on getting started with storage and indexing In terms of storage and indexing, I'd imagine we could start off by storing the notebook blobs in S3, relying on Object versioning

`AVOID_MULTIVALUED_READS` and `AVOID_MULTIVALUED_WRITES` cause angr to, instead of carrying out symbolic reads or rights, return fresh symbolic variables. My guess for what's going on is the following:

Claripy currently processes ASTs recursively (easier to write), so very complex ASTs will cause recursion errors in Python. I am pretty sure that this is what's happening here, rather than an actual endless loop. The `AVOID_MULTIVALUED_READS` and `AVOID_MULTIVALUED_WRITES` options just cause very tiny (but also not actually correct, as the relationships are lost) ASTs to be used instead, so this also avoids the core issue (though the result is wrong in this case). The correct solution is to do one (or both) of two things:

1. Build in some functionality in angr to create a new symbolic variable to represent ASTs that get to a depth that's too big. This would have a similar effect, in this case, to the `AVOID_MULTIVALUED` options, but would maintain correctness. This could be done ad-hoc as a SimInspect hook, btw, similar to your simplifiers.
2. Look at the operation and see what can be simplified. Usually, an AST that hits the recursion limit is doing something dumb over and over again, and it can be optimized away. The place to put this in would be the simplification functions here: https://github.com/angr/claripy/blob/master/claripy/operations.py

Your simplifier looks pretty good, btw (based on a quick look!). I think it would be a great addition to Claripy, and should simplify a decent amount of ASTs in other situations as well. In general, I guess, there are a lot of places where we could improve our simplifications for `Reverse`. If you could add your simplification (and a testcase for it) to claripy, we would be very thankful!

The problem has something to do with the regex, which also explains why it works on Linux but not on Windows.

On Windows, `exename` is set to `mpv.exe` or `mpv.com`. This will work for getting the subprocess output. The problem is that `mpsyt` tries to match for `exename` in the `--version` output as well. As the version output does not contain this extension, there are no matches:

```

As you can see, it only contains 'mpv'. On Linux, this is no problem: the exename is `mpv`, too.

Proposed solution: declare a different variable, without the extension.

I do notice that Gym has [a learning demo](https://github.com/openai/gym/blob/master/examples/agents/cem.py), but it's totally self-contained. If we have a learning demo for Retro, perhaps we should find an easy game and implement a simple, self-contained algorithm for it. Otherwise, these two issues might arise:

 * Baselines doesn't guarantee a non-breaking API, so changes to baselines might break the Retro demo.
 * Pretty much any off-the-shelf RL implementation is going to be terrible at most Retro games.

Beyond the above points, I think this specific DQN is a bit misleading. It does a lot of things that you wouldn't actually want to do for a general Retro agent:

 * Uses an MLP model, which is likely to be much worse than a CNN.
 * Doesn't take reward scale into account. Different games have vastly different reward scales. DQN will not do well with games where rewards are large (e.g. in the thousands, or even hundreds). This is a non-trivial issue to fix, especially since rewards can start out small but then get large in certain games. Reward clipping, the common fix for DQN on Atari, is a hacky solution and won't work for many of the Retro games.
 * Assumes a hard-coded level of reward for termination (see previous point).

When we launched, the way we approached was very similar to SQLAlchemy/Django.

You have to create a Field and then a Type, like:
```python
class User(graphene.ObjectType):
    name = graphene.StringField()
```

But the main usage problem arises when you want to use a String as a Argument too (not only as a Field). So naming `StringField` for and `Argument` was not very intuitive.
So then, we switched to:

```python
class User(graphene.ObjectType):
    name = graphene.Field(graphene.String(), my_arg=graphene.Argument(graphene.String()))
```

However we were not as confortable as we should with this last approach, as is much more verbose.

Apart from that, this approach was working for `SQLAlchemy` as mostly the only time you use a type is in a `Column` function, however in `GraphQL` a field is another kind of type.
So we decided to simplify to this:

```python
class User(graphene.ObjectType):
    name = graphene.String(my_arg=graphene.String())
```

Which is much more easier to read and develop, so we adapted the code internally for make it work.
We also make sure that the previous way -and more verbose- was also working.

However if you have idea that could make it better I'm quite open for discussing and improving `graphene` for the benefit of all :)

viz = visdom.Visdom()

))

> One thing I should point out, though, is that Eigen itself will make a copy if you try to pass a storage-incompatible matrix into a `Ref<const M>`, so we're not really doing something totally unexpected here.

AFAIK The only time `Ref<const M>` is allowed to create a temporary is when accepting an expression. Then it evaluates that expression into a compatible format. But if it's assigned a concrete matrix, it's not going to accept incompatible strides.

in `mail-parser` results there is a `binary` flag. If it's `true` the sample is a binary, it's `false` is not binary.
If you read this [part of code](https://github.com/SpamScope/mail-parser/blob/develop/mailparser/mailparser.py#L335) you can see:

```
...
charset = p.get_content_charset('utf-8') # get the charset
....

            if filename:
                    binary = False
                    mail_content_type = ported_string(p.get_content_type())
                    transfer_encoding = ported_string(
                        p.get('content-transfer-encoding', '')).lower()

                    if transfer_encoding == "base64" or \
                            (transfer_encoding == "quoted-printable" and
                             "application" in mail_content_type):
                        payload = p.get_payload(decode=False)
                        binary = True
                    else:
                        payload = ported_string(
                            p.get_payload(decode=True), encoding=charset)
```
`mail-parser` gives you the correct payload, so you should do:

```
            if binary:
                with open(sample, "wb") as f:
                    f.write(payload.decode("base64"))
            else:
                with open(sample, "w") as f:
                    f.write(payload.encode("utf-8"))
```

ok, we've discussed with Stephanie that we should do a more graceful shutdown of workers when we try to kill or evict them. It's best to send the worker a SIGTERM signal and implement a SIGTERM handler in the worker to allow the worker to close all of its sockets. In the meantime, the local scheduler schedules a timer to send a followup SIGKILL to the evicted worker after 1ms. The reason for this is that, as things are right now, the worker ends up in an inconsistent state and attempts to communicate on sockets that are still open, fails and generates core files while failing. This is reproduceable on a Mac with num_workers >= 10 . Example core dump:

```

First of all, I have no idea where you get that "used by discord literally" from, but if that was the case, I fail to see why Discord still shows the correct syntax when you do:
```
```py
print("py")```
```
If you try that right now on Discord, you'll see the string is indeed highlighted and, more importantly, that the line is actually there.

Secondly, the first line's matching seems to work more like:
- Find code block by looking for pairs of triple backticks
- Get the first line of the code block
- If there's multiple lines and the first line contains only a word, remove that line and try to find a syntax for that language

This can be checked by trying any valid syntax highlight by your definition and just adding a space (or any non-alphanumeric character for that matter; try with `py=hi` (displayed) and `py3` (not displayed) as your language specifier) after the syntax specifier.

This makes me think Discord actually finds code blocks to display the text in a monospace font and adds the little fancy rectangle around it and then looks for a syntax specifier to decide whether it displays the first line or not. It seems to do this by checking whether or not the first line contains non-alphanumerical characters (as demonstrated by `py=hi` and `py3` which both don't display colors). When it finally finds a syntax specifier, it tries to look up the actual syntax to decide where to put colors.

Thirdly, your "used by Discord literally" doesn't quite work since having exactly zero lines between the syntax specifier and the closing backticks actually displays the syntax specifier. This further reinforces the idea that Discord first finds code blocks just by looking for pairs of triple backticks and then tries to match the syntax. When it does find the language, it removes the line and colors the text, but when it doesn't, it keeps displaying code blocks as usual.

Finally, I'd like to say the command should at the very least provide a reasonable error message (and not a cryptic SyntaxError which happens because of how the code is parsed by the command). After that, there's no loss in trying a little harder to guess what the user expects. I'm pretty sure nobody expects the bot to completely wipe the first and last lines of the code block when they can see those lines in the command's message on Discord.

As a solution, I suggest this little bit of not too complicated code to properly handle the code blocks the way Discord seems to display them:
```py
def cleanup_code(content):
    # remove ```\n```
    if content.startswith("```") and content.endswith("```"):
        content = content[3:-3]
        lines = content.split("\n")
        if lines[0].isalnum():
            del lines[0]
        return "\n".join(lines)

    # remove `foo`
    return content.strip("` \n")
```
This is actually not enough to solve all the problems I've talked about in this comment, the command should also check whether the cleaned coded is full of whitespace or not and give an appropriate error message if it's indeed only whitespace.

This also doesn't solve the cases where there's nothing else than the syntax specifier in the code block, but that would be picked up by the aforementioned check.

Note: If you actually try what you wrote in the end about `py` being used as a variable and the syntax specifier, you can notice `py` isn't a valid syntax specifier in that case which means it's only used as a variable which makes sense when you look at the displayed code block. This case is properly handled by the suggested code.

That's a valid approach, but I'd rather move more towards a quick-release low-ceremony model (like we discussed some in #220), and the canonical way to get our latest release is to check PyPI, so a `release` branch is redundant (violates DRY). So... I don't think having a release branch provides much value here.

@igordejanovic That's an awsome news! I updated my fork, and modify the sources so it matches well the new textX code.

Everything's running fine at the moment, but there is some existing tests I need to modify as there is some rules that works well with textX but which needs to be rewritten when PyEcore is used (as stated in a previous message). Also, the typing system for PyEcore is a little bit different as `EClass` are instance that behave like classes, but they are not classes per say (PyEcore builds an instance level representation for each `EClass` instance, and it keeps a bound to a Python class that it updates, [you can see an example here](https://modeling-languages.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/02/img_58993c2977676.png)) . This implies that some assert like:

```python
assert type(obj) is MyMetaclass
```
Must be changed to:

```python
assert obj.eClass is MyMetaclass 
# or
assert type(obj) is MyMetaclass.python_class
```
when the metamodel is build in memory by textX. When a static metamodel is used, the first assertion works just fine.

Beside that, everything is good on my side :). I refactored a little bit the code so textX + PyEcore can cohabitate with the original textX version. I will try to make all the tests running fine on the fork, then refactor the code, and try to integrate everything as it should.

The same filename  appears many times in the cache file. The file is added to the cache as soon as the upload starts. If the file is very large  and the poll is running every 5 seconds, it means the file will be added to the cache every 5 seconds as long as the file is being uploaded, this could filling up the cache file for the poll that runs so often if the the files are very large.
